papers.title: A systematic benchmark of bioinformatics methods for single-cell and spatial RNA-seq nanopore long reads data.
papers.abstract: Alternative splicing plays a crucial role in transcriptomic complexity, yet remains difficult to resolve at the single-cell level due to the limitations of short-read technologies. Coupling single-cell with long-read sequencing offers full-length transcript coverage, enabling more accurate isoform detection. Diverse computational tools tailored for single-cell and spatial long-read transcriptomics have been developed. To compare the effectiveness of these approaches, we generated paired short-read and Nanopore long-read single-cell datasets, tailored for benchmarking bioinformatics tools. We e
papers.summary: This study benchmarks computational tools for analyzing long-read single-cell and spatial RNA-seq data, which can capture full-length transcripts and better resolve alternative splicing than short-read methods. The authors generated paired short-read and Nanopore long-read datasets to systematically compare the performance of various bioinformatics approaches. The findings provide guidance for selecting appropriate tools for isoform detection in single-cell and spatial transcriptomics.

papers.title: Multi-omics identification of an antibody dependent cellular phagocytosis related signature for hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: The reduced phagocytic activity of macrophages in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and the presence of anti-phagocytic factors in cancer cells hinder the effectiveness of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in clinical settings. Consequently, our objective was to create a risk assessment model using antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP) regulators to serve as prognostic indicators and immunotherapy markers for individuals with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
papers.summary: This study developed a risk assessment model based on antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP) regulators to predict prognosis and immunotherapy response in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) patients. By integrating multi-omics data, the authors identified a signature that correlates with macrophage phagocytic activity in the tumor microenvironment, potentially improving the effectiveness of immune checkpoint inhibitors.

papers.title: Single-cell mitophagy patterns dictate intercellular crosstalk in the tumor microenvironment to promote osteosarcoma progression.
papers.abstract: Osteosarcoma (OS) is a highly aggressive primary bone malignancy in adolescents, with poor prognosis due to limited diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Mitochondrial dysfunction is a hallmark of cancer, and mitophagy, the selective clearance of damaged mitochondria, critically maintains cellular homeostasis. However, the specific role of mitophagy in shaping the OS tumor microenvironment (TME) at single-cell resolution remains poorly understood. This study aims to systematically characterize mitophagy patterns within the OS TME and investigate their impact on intercellular communication, tu
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell analysis to map mitophagy patterns in osteosarcoma, revealing that different mitophagy states in tumor cells influence communication with immune and stromal cells in the tumor microenvironment. The findings suggest that targeting mitophagy could disrupt pro-tumor signaling and improve treatment strategies for this aggressive bone cancer.

papers.title: A Simple, Cost-Effective, High-Throughput Method for Measuring Chromatin Accessibility and Gene Expression in Single Nuclei.
papers.abstract: We describe microfluidic-free, droplet-based methods for single-nucleus epigenomic measurements: Particle-templated Instant Partition single-nucleus assay for transposase-accessible chromatin using sequencing (PIP-ATAC-seq) and its multiomic version (PIP-Multiome-seq). We benchmarked these assays by generating data sets containing thousands of nuclei using cell lines and mouse brains and compared to other established methods. PIP-Multiome and PIP-ATAC are straightforward to implement, affordable, and produce high-quality data, providing useful additions to the single-cell molecular measurement
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces PIP-ATAC-seq and PIP-Multiome-seq, new microfluidic-free, droplet-based methods for measuring chromatin accessibility and gene expression in single nuclei. These methods are cost-effective, easy to implement, and produce high-quality data comparable to established techniques, making them valuable tools for single-cell epigenomic research.

papers.title: Immunothrombotic Cell-Cell Communication Networks in Coronary Atherosclerosis: Critical Insights from Single-Cell and Spatial Systems Biology.
papers.abstract: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is increasingly recognized as a thromboinflammatory disorder in which innate immune activation and coagulation are tightly coupled within the plaque microenvironment. Emerging single-cell and spatial technologies have refined this paradigm by demonstrating that these processes are not diffusely distributed but instead concentrated within discrete cellular niches. This narrative review critically evaluates mechanistic and translational studies integrating single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and ligand-receptor modeling to characterize cell-cell com
papers.summary: This review synthesizes findings from single-cell and spatial studies showing that immune activation and coagulation in coronary artery disease are concentrated in discrete cellular niches rather than being diffusely distributed. The authors critically evaluate how single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and ligand-receptor modeling have refined our understanding of cell-cell communication networks driving immunothrombosis in atherosclerosis.

papers.title: SDF4: a novel prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in hepatocellular carcinoma revealed by integrated multi‑omics analysis.
papers.abstract: SDF4 is a calcium-binding protein with limited research in cancer, particularly liver hepatocellular carcinoma (LIHC). This study aims to clarify its expression and prognostic significance in LIHC, explore its regulatory effects on the tumor microenvironment (TME), and assess its relationship with drug sensitivity for new diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
papers.summary: This study identifies SDF4 as a potential prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in liver cancer by analyzing its expression and role in the tumor microenvironment. The findings suggest that SDF4 could be used for new diagnostic and treatment strategies in hepatocellular carcinoma.

papers.title: Lineage-aware stochastic modeling reveals gene-expression dynamics in development and disease.
papers.abstract: Gene expression evolves dynamically along cell lineages, yet most analysis methods treat single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) data as static snapshots and fail to exploit phylogenetic relationships among cells. Recent advances in cell-lineage tracing now enable the reconstruction of high-resolution lineage phylogenies, providing a natural framework for identifying when and where transcriptional changes arise during development, differentiation, and disease progression. Some models of gene expression have begun to consider phylogenetic structure, but they generally rely on imprecise Gaussian assumpt
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces a lineage-aware stochastic model that leverages phylogenetic relationships from cell-lineage tracing to analyze gene expression dynamics in single-cell RNA-seq data, moving beyond static snapshot approaches. By incorporating precise stochastic modeling rather than Gaussian assumptions, the method identifies when and where transcriptional changes occur during development and disease progression. This approach provides a more accurate framework for understanding gene-expression dynamics along cell lineages.

papers.title: Statistical Methods for Detecting Nonlinear Relationships in Gene Expression and Omics Data: A Review.
papers.abstract: High-throughput technologies such as RNA-seq and single-cell transcriptomics generate increasingly large and high-dimensional gene expression datasets in which nonlinear dependence structures are common. Because classical methods primarily capture linear associations, they may fail to characterize many biologically relevant patterns of dependence. To address this limitation, diverse nonlinear dependence measures-including information-theoretic, rank-based, kernel-based, distance-based, copula-based, and clustering-based approaches-have been developed. However, the field remains fragmented, and
papers.summary: This review examines statistical methods for detecting nonlinear relationships in high-dimensional gene expression and omics data, covering approaches such as information-theoretic, kernel-based, and copula-based measures. It highlights the limitations of classical linear methods for capturing complex biological patterns and aims to organize the fragmented field of nonlinear dependence measures.

papers.title: Single-Cell Omics Advances in Understanding Tissue Development and Complex Trait Formation in Sheep and Goats.
papers.abstract: Single-cell omics technologies have transformed the study of cellular heterogeneity, enabling high-resolution analysis of tissue development and complex traits. In sheep and goats, these approaches have been applied to skin, hair follicles, reproductive organs, metabolic tissues, and adipose tissue, revealing cell type-specific regulatory programs underlying traits such as wool quality, fertility, growth, and fat deposition. However, most studies rely on single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and are limited by incomplete genome annotation, insufficient coverage of production traits, and weak
papers.journal: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
papers.summary: This review discusses how single-cell omics technologies, particularly scRNA-seq, have been used to study tissue development and complex traits in sheep and goats, revealing cell-specific gene programs for wool quality, fertility, growth, and fat deposition. It highlights current limitations such as incomplete genome annotations and a narrow focus on a few traits, suggesting future directions for broader application.

papers.title: The Serpin Superfamily in Adipose Tissue Remodeling: Molecular Drivers of Immune-Metabolic Crosstalk and Insulin Sensitivity.
papers.abstract: Adipose tissue remodeling is a dynamic process essential for metabolic homeostasis, enabling tissue expansion, extracellular matrix (ECM) turnover, angiogenesis, and coordinated immune adaptation. In obesity, however, maladaptive remodeling characterized by fibrosis, chronic low-grade inflammation, and hypoxia disrupts adipose plasticity and promotes systemic insulin resistance. Central to these processes is the tightly regulated homeostasis between proteases and their inhibitors, in which the serine protease inhibitor (serpin) superfamily represents an important yet underappreciated regulator
papers.summary: This review highlights the role of the serpin superfamily in regulating adipose tissue remodeling, focusing on how these protease inhibitors control extracellular matrix turnover, inflammation, and insulin sensitivity. The authors propose that serpins are key molecular drivers of immune-metabolic crosstalk in adipose tissue, and their dysregulation in obesity contributes to fibrosis and insulin resistance.

papers.title: pyVIPER: a fast and scalable Python package for protein activity estimation and master regulator analysis of single-cell RNA sequencing data.
papers.abstract: Single-cell sequencing has revolutionized biomedical research by offering insights into cellular heterogeneity at unprecedented resolution. Yet, the low signal-to-noise ratio characteristic of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) challenges quantitative analyses. Gene regulatory network (GRN) analysis can help overcome this obstacle, enabling the mechanistic elucidation of cellular state determinants. For instance, the VIPER algorithm can identify Master Regulator proteins from gene expression data. However, as the size and complexity of scRNA-seq datasets grow, the demand for scalable tools
papers.summary: pyVIPER is a new Python package that adapts the VIPER algorithm for large-scale single-cell RNA sequencing data, enabling fast and scalable estimation of protein activity and identification of master regulator proteins. It addresses the computational bottleneck of analyzing increasingly large scRNA-seq datasets while maintaining the mechanistic insights provided by gene regulatory network analysis.

papers.title: Spatially informed reference-free cell-type deconvolution for spatial transcriptomics with SpatialCD.
papers.abstract: Cell-type deconvolution has been instrumental for the analysis of spatial transcriptomics (ST) data to reveal underlying tissue heterogeneity. Although reference-based methods have been widely explored, practical limitations, particularly the need for matched single-cell RNA-seq data sets, highlight the value of robust reference-free methods. Existing reference-free approaches, such as STdeconvolve, overlook spatial information, despite the well-established observation that spatially adjacent spots often share similar cellular compositions. Motivated by this, we propose SpatialCD, a spatially
papers.summary: SpatialCD is a new computational method that improves cell-type deconvolution in spatial transcriptomics without needing a reference single-cell RNA-seq dataset. By incorporating spatial information from neighboring spots, it outperforms existing reference-free methods like STdeconvolve.

papers.title: Condition-Associated Pattern Extraction and Recovery From Multi-Condition Single-Cell RNA-seq Data With CAPER.
papers.abstract: A central challenge in multi-condition single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data analysis is the disentanglement of true biological signals from unwanted variations in complex experimental designs. Current statistical and machine learning-based methods struggle with this task, often providing only visualizable embeddings, over-correcting and discarding biological signal, or failing to resolve cell-type-specific responses. Here, we present CAPER, a matrix factorization framework that explicitly disentangles shared biological states from condition-specific variations. CAPER directly outputs an
papers.summary: CAPER is a new computational framework that disentangles true biological signals from unwanted variations in multi-condition single-cell RNA-seq data, overcoming limitations of existing methods that often over-correct or fail to capture cell-type-specific responses. By using matrix factorization, it explicitly separates shared biological states from condition-specific variations, enabling more accurate recovery of condition-associated patterns.

papers.title: Comprehensive assessment of alternative splicing analysis methods for single-cell RNA-seq.
papers.abstract: Alternative splicing expands transcriptomic and proteomic diversity and contributes to cell identity and function. Advances in single-cell RNA sequencing enable transcriptome profiling at cellular resolution, yet their performance across biologically relevant tasks has not been systematically evaluated. Here, we benchmark six representative alternative splicing analysis methods across multiple full-length, short-read scRNA-seq datasets, focusing on biologically relevant tasks including cell clustering and differential splicing detection. Among these methods, scQuint achieves robust performance
papers.summary: This study benchmarks six computational methods for analyzing alternative splicing in single-cell RNA-seq data, finding that scQuint performs best for tasks like cell clustering and detecting differential splicing. The work provides guidance for researchers choosing tools to study how splicing contributes to cell identity and function.

papers.title: Interpretable multi-omics integration across mixed-order tensors with MANTRA.
papers.abstract: The integration of multi-modal molecular data is crucial for understanding complex diseases, but existing methods struggle with modern experimental designs that generate datasets with mixed-order tensors-for example, a third-order drug-response tensor alongside a second-order transcriptomics matrix. Here, we present MANTRA (Multi-view ANalysis with Tensor and matRix Alignment), a probabilistic framework that integrates collections of tensors of different orders, combining the strengths of group factor analysis and tensor decomposition. MANTRA learns interpretable latent factors and naturally h
papers.summary: MANTRA is a new computational method that integrates different types of molecular data, such as drug-response measurements (3D tensor) and gene expression data (2D matrix), into a unified analysis. It combines group factor analysis and tensor decomposition to learn interpretable latent factors, making it easier to understand complex diseases from multi-omics datasets.

papers.title: Annotation-Based Gene-Peak Links Improve Regulatory Network Prediction of Gene Expression in Human Kidney Multi-Omics.
papers.abstract: Linking distal regulatory elements to their target genes is a central problem for interpreting chromatin accessibility and other non-coding genomic data. Proximity-based mapping is convenient but ignores three-dimensional enhancer-promoter architecture and can misassign long-range regulatory effects. Correlation-based approaches can also miss regulatory links because of limited statistical power and restrictive distance or significance thresholds. Single-cell and single-nucleus multi-omic datasets, such as 10x Multiome profiles that jointly measure chromatin accessibility and gene expression i
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study presents a new method for linking distal regulatory elements to their target genes by using annotation-based gene-peak links, which improves the prediction of gene expression from chromatin accessibility data. Applied to human kidney multi-omics data, the approach outperforms proximity-based and correlation-based methods, particularly in capturing long-range regulatory interactions. The findings offer a more accurate framework for interpreting non-coding genomic data in complex tissues.

papers.title: CAdir: Joint clustering of cells and genes for single-cell transcriptomics with visualization-driven cluster quality assessment.
papers.abstract: Clustering for single-cell RNA-seq aims at finding similar cells and grouping them into biologically meaningful clusters. Many available clustering algorithms however do not not provide the cluster defining marker genes or are unable to infer the number of clusters in an unsupervised manner as well as lack tools to easily determine the quality of the label assignments. Therefore, clustering quality is commonly evaluated by visually inspecting low-dimensional embeddings as produced by, e.g., UMAP or t-SNE. These embeddings can, however, distort the true cluster structure and are known to produc
papers.summary: CAdir is a new method for single-cell RNA-seq clustering that simultaneously clusters cells and identifies marker genes, and includes a visualization-driven tool to assess cluster quality. It addresses common limitations of existing algorithms by inferring the number of clusters unsupervised and providing a way to evaluate label assignment quality without relying solely on potentially misleading UMAP/t-SNE embeddings.

papers.title: Use of organ transplant solution to preserve skeletal muscle for cellular and spatial transcriptomic analyses.
papers.abstract: Single-cell and spatial transcriptomic technologies have revolutionized human cell and tissue research. Success of these methods critically depends on the preservation of RNA integrity, cellular viability, and structure of the explanted tissues-features that are impacted by tissue storage conditions, and pose challenges in research settings, where tissues are often collected in the operating room and transported to distant laboratories. In this study, we evaluated the effectiveness of a clinically approved organ transplant solution (TS) for preserving skeletal muscle under refrigerated conditi
papers.summary: This study tests whether a clinically approved organ transplant solution can preserve skeletal muscle tissue for single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, finding it maintains RNA integrity and cellular structure under refrigerated conditions. This offers a practical solution for transporting surgical tissue samples to distant labs without compromising molecular analysis quality.

papers.title: ViMST: vision transformer-based dual modality multi-task graph contrastive network for spatial transcriptomics microenvironments investigation.
papers.abstract: Investigating spatial transcriptomics microenvironments is crucial for unraveling cellular heterogeneity. Existing methods struggle to extract non-redundant information from histopathological images, as well as to simultaneously and spatially resolve gene expression profiles. We propose a vision transformer-based dual-modality multi-task graph contrastive network for exploring the spatial transcriptomics domain (ViMST), which integrates gene expression, image features, and spatial coordinates to investigate tissue microenvironments. It employs Vision Transformer (ViT) for feature extraction an
papers.summary: ViMST is a new computational method that combines gene expression, tissue image features, and spatial coordinates to better analyze spatial transcriptomics data. It uses a Vision Transformer to extract features from histology images and a multi-task graph contrastive network to integrate these data types, improving the identification of tissue microenvironments and cellular heterogeneity.

papers.title: Cellular hallmarks and aging clock of the human lung parenchyma.
papers.abstract: Aging affects lung function, predisposing older adults to respiratory diseases; however, the cellular and molecular mechanisms of lung aging are not fully understood. Leveraging single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data from 184 and 70 lung parenchyma samples, respectively, we present an analytical platform to dissect the cell composition, gene expression modules, and regulatory changes linked to multiple hallmarks of lung aging. Our findings show cell type-specific age-association of senescence markers and a decline in alveolar cell proliferation, autocrine WNT signaling, and stemness indi
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data from hundreds of human lung samples to characterize how aging affects lung tissue at the cellular level. The authors identify cell type-specific changes in senescence markers, reduced alveolar cell proliferation, and declines in WNT signaling and stemness, providing a detailed molecular aging clock for the lung parenchyma.

papers.title: Multiomic profiling of responses to clinical and novel bisphosphonates reveals extraskeletal effects on ageing related signatures.
papers.abstract: Bisphosphonates (BPs) have been used effectively to treat excessive bone loss for over 50 years. Recent clinical evidence suggests extra-skeletal benefits but how this occurs remains unknown. Here we use a panel of human, murine and cellular assessments to chart BP-induced ageing-related changes both systemically and at local organ sites. In vivo spatial transcriptomics in aged mice treated with zoledronate showed a shift in cellular composition towards that of young animals specifically in heart, liver and intestine, with upregulation of genes governing detoxification, mitochondrial stability
papers.summary: This study shows that bisphosphonates, drugs traditionally used to treat bone loss, also have anti-aging effects on other organs. Using spatial transcriptomics in aged mice, the researchers found that treatment with the bisphosphonate zoledronate shifted gene expression and cellular composition in the heart, liver, and intestine toward patterns seen in young animals, particularly upregulating genes related to detoxification and mitochondrial stability.

papers.title: Single-cell and spatial multi-omics reveal mechanistic insights of FOLH1+ endothelial cells in clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of renal malignancy, characterized by extensive vascularization and poor prognosis. Early detection markedly improves patient outcomes, and prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA, encoded by FOLH1) imaging has recently emerged as a valuable diagnostic tool for ccRCC. However, the cellular source, spatial distribution, and mechanistic contribution of FOLH1 expression within the tumor microenvironment remain poorly understood.
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial multi-omics to identify FOLH1+ endothelial cells in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), revealing their cellular source and spatial distribution within the tumor microenvironment. The findings provide mechanistic insights into how these cells may contribute to tumor vascularization and poor prognosis, and support the use of PSMA imaging as a diagnostic tool for ccRCC.

papers.title: Single-cell transcriptome reveals keratinocyte subclusters contributing to altered differentiation and inflammatory responses in atopic dermatitis.
papers.abstract: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a common chronic inflammatory skin disease with heterogeneous and poorly understood mechanisms. We perform single-cell RNA sequencing of lesional (LAD) and non-lesional (NAD) skin from 42 AD patients and 23 healthy controls (HC). Keratinocytes (KCs) are the predominant disease-relevant cell type. In healthy skin, KC differentiation follows a linear trajectory from basal KCs through seven differentiated stages to terminal keratinized cells. In LAD, this process is disrupted, showing a reversed terminal transition mainly driven by DK7 cells. APOD, LYZ and SERPINB4 emerg
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell RNA sequencing of skin from atopic dermatitis patients and healthy controls to identify keratinocyte subclusters that disrupt normal differentiation and drive inflammatory responses. The authors find that in lesional skin, the terminal differentiation of keratinocytes is reversed, primarily driven by a specific cell type (DK7 cells), and identify new marker genes (APOD, LYZ, SERPINB4) associated with this process.

papers.title: Conserved anterior/posterior tissue asymmetry encodes proximal/distal positional information during axolotl limb regeneration.
papers.abstract: While mammalian limb regeneration is limited to the distal fingertips, axolotls can regenerate entire limbs, providing a model system to uncover conserved patterning mechanisms. Although anterior-posterior (A/P) signaling is required for limb regeneration, how A/P patterning is coordinated across limb segments remains unclear. Using spatial transcriptomics and our SpatialFlux analysis pipeline, we found that A/P genes are asymmetrically distributed along the proximal-distal (Pr/Di) axis of both uninjured and regenerating axolotl limbs. This asymmetric boundary coincides with distally enriched
papers.summary: This study reveals that anterior-posterior (A/P) gene expression is asymmetrically distributed along the proximal-distal (Pr/Di) axis in axolotl limbs, providing positional information for regeneration. Using spatial transcriptomics and a new analysis pipeline, the authors show this conserved asymmetry helps coordinate limb segment patterning during regeneration.

papers.title: Single-Cell and Spatial Analysis Reveal the Differences Between Left-Sided and Right-Sided Colorectal Cancer.
papers.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) exhibits heterogeneity based on tumour laterality, with right-sided tumours demonstrating distinct genetic, immunogenic and clinical behaviours compared to left-sided counterparts. In this study, we conducted single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics to dissect the distinct molecular landscapes of left- and right-sided CRC. We identified enhanced germinal centre B cell infiltration and CXCL13+ T cell enrichment, features linked to adaptive immune priming and better immunotherapy responsiveness. Right-sided CRC epithelial cells exhibited proliferative and im
papers.summary: This study used single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics to compare left-sided and right-sided colorectal cancer. The researchers found that right-sided tumors have more germinal center B cells and CXCL13+ T cells, which are linked to better immunotherapy responses, while their epithelial cells are more proliferative. These findings help explain the clinical differences between the two tumor locations.

papers.title: Single-cell and spatial transcriptomic analysis reveal distinct tumor microenvironment signatures in primary and recurrent hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (HPSCC) is a rare, highly aggressive malignancy, with recurrence playing a pivotal role in treatment failure and cancer-related mortality. The tumor microenvironment (TME) characteristics and molecular mechanisms driving recurrence in HPSCC remain poorly defined.
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to compare the tumor microenvironments of primary and recurrent hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, revealing distinct cellular and molecular signatures associated with recurrence. The findings provide new insights into the mechanisms driving treatment failure and suggest potential targets for therapy.

papers.title: Spatial and single-cell profiling identify NDRG1⁺ macrophages as key hallmark of angiogenic remodeling and platinum resistance in HGSOC.
papers.abstract: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) exhibits profound immunologic and metabolic remodeling, yet the macrophage programs that drive disease progression and therapeutic resistance remain incompletely defined. Here, we integrate spatial transcriptomics, single-cell sequencing, bulk deconvolution, pseudotime reconstruction, and multiplex immunofluorescence to delineate a distinct tumor-promoting macrophage phenotype, termed Macro4, characterized by hypoxia adaptation, extracellular matrix remodeling, and pro-angiogenic signaling. Macro4 signature-based RSF model robustly predicted poor surviv
papers.summary: This study identifies a distinct macrophage subtype (Macro4) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer that is characterized by hypoxia adaptation, extracellular matrix remodeling, and pro-angiogenic signaling, and shows that this subtype is associated with platinum resistance and poor survival. The findings were validated using spatial transcriptomics, single-cell sequencing, and multiplex immunofluorescence, and a predictive model based on the Macro4 signature was developed.

papers.title: Multi-omics analysis of arginine metabolism in ovarian cancer: A prognostic signature and GTF2F2-driven stromal remodeling.
papers.abstract: Arginine metabolism shapes tumor growth, stromal activation, and antitumor immunity, yet its translational relevance in ovarian cancer (OV) remains incompletely defined. We sought to derive an arginine-metabolism-related signature (AMRS) for prognosis and therapy guidance and to nominate actionable regulators.
papers.summary: This study develops a prognostic signature based on arginine metabolism genes in ovarian cancer, showing that high-risk patients have worse outcomes and distinct immune microenvironments. The transcription factor GTF2F2 is identified as a key regulator that drives stromal remodeling and may represent a therapeutic target.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics identifies CYP1B1 as a candidate molecular link between toxicant exposure and ferroptosis-related epithelial stress in COPD.
papers.abstract: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is characterized by progressive airflow limitation, chronic airway inflammation, and epithelial remodeling. Although oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation are increasingly recognized as important contributors to COPD pathobiology, the epithelial biomarkers and regulatory pathways linking cigarette-smoke exposure to ferroptosis-related injury remain incompletely defined.
papers.journal: Inflammation research : official journal of the European Histamine Research Society ... [et al.]
papers.summary: This study uses an integrative multi-omics approach to identify CYP1B1 as a potential molecular link between cigarette smoke exposure and ferroptosis-related epithelial stress in COPD. The findings suggest that CYP1B1 could serve as a biomarker and therapeutic target for COPD by connecting toxicant exposure to ferroptosis-driven airway epithelial injury.

papers.title: Spatial metabolomics - a metabolic microscope for respiratory medicine.
papers.abstract: Spatial metabolomics enables in situ, pixel-resolved mapping of small molecules across tissues, providing a powerful complement to conventional bulk metabolomics, which lacks cellular and anatomical resolution. By adapting mass spectrometry (MS) ionization approaches such as matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) and desorption electrospray ionization (DESI), spatial metabolomics generates high-resolution metabolic maps that link metabolite distributions to defined tissue regions, cellular niches, and biological functions. This "metabolic microscopy" enables localization of biosyn
papers.journal: American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology
papers.summary: This review introduces spatial metabolomics as a technique that maps small molecules across tissues with high resolution, complementing traditional bulk metabolomics. By using mass spectrometry methods like MALDI and DESI, it links metabolite distributions to specific tissue regions and cellular functions, offering a 'metabolic microscope' for respiratory medicine.

papers.title: Integrative single-cell and spatial transcriptomic analysis reveals a lactate-driven crosstalk between NFATc4⁺ tumor cells and SPP1⁺ macrophages in glioblastoma.
papers.abstract: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains a lethal brain tumor with limited therapeutic options. Metabolic reprogramming, particularly lactate metabolism, plays a critical role in tumor progression and immune evasion.
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to uncover how lactate in glioblastoma drives communication between a specific subtype of tumor cells (NFATc4⁺) and immune cells (SPP1⁺ macrophages), promoting tumor growth and immune evasion. The findings identify a new mechanism of metabolic crosstalk in the tumor microenvironment that could be targeted for therapy.

papers.title: Advancing genomics and integration of multi-omics for precision oncology using quantum machine learning.
papers.abstract: Cancer multi-omics faces challenges in handling the scale, complexity, and heterogeneity of multi-omics data, limiting progress in variant interpretation, tumor classification, and modeling cancer evolution. Quantum computing offers a new paradigm using superposition, entanglement, and quantum interference to efficiently explore vast solution spaces. This Perspective highlights how quantum algorithms-such as Quantum Support Vector Machines, Quantum Principal Component Analysis, and quantum generative models-could enhance key tasks in precision oncology, including multi-omics integration, spati
papers.summary: This perspective article discusses how quantum machine learning algorithms could address key challenges in precision oncology, such as integrating multi-omics data and improving tumor classification. The authors propose that quantum computing's unique properties like superposition and entanglement may enable more efficient analysis of complex, heterogeneous cancer data compared to classical methods.

papers.title: Single-Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics Unveil Key Regulators Governing Cell Differentiation for Schistosoma japonicum Sexual Development.
papers.abstract: Schistosomiasis remains a major global health challenge, primarily due to the large number of egg production by sexually mature parasites. However, the cellular basis and regulatory programs governing sexual development and egg production are poorly characterized. Here, we constructed a dynamic single-cell atlas of Schistosoma japonicum (S. japonicum) covering key development stages of sexual maturation and egg production, and integrated it for the first time with spatial transcriptomics to map tissue‑resolved cellular niches. Through bioinformatic analysis and experimental validation, we iden
papers.summary: This study constructs a dynamic single-cell atlas of the parasitic flatworm Schistosoma japonicum across key stages of sexual maturation and egg production, integrating it with spatial transcriptomics to map tissue-resolved cellular niches. The authors identify key regulators governing cell differentiation during sexual development, providing new insights into the cellular basis of egg production, which is the primary driver of schistosomiasis pathology.

papers.title: Loss of Neuropeptide Y Signaling Accompanies the Neural-to-Mesenchymal Transcriptional Transition in Glioblastoma: A Multi-Scale Transcriptomic Analysis.
papers.abstract: Neuropeptide Y [NPY; encoded by the NPY gene] is a widely expressed 36-amino-acid neuropeptide that regulates neuronal function, vascular regulation, and immune regulation; its role in glioblastoma [GBM] remains incompletely characterized. We performed an integrative in silico multi-scale transcriptomic analysis combining bulk RNA-sequencing of IDH-wildtype GBM [n = 169] and lower-grade glioma [n = 510] surgical resections from TCGA, normal cortical tissue from GTEx [n = 207], and four independent GEO validation cohorts of surgical GBM and non-tumor brain specimens [GSE4290, GSE50161, GSE13192
papers.summary: This study uses multi-scale transcriptomic analysis to show that Neuropeptide Y (NPY) signaling is lost during the neural-to-mesenchymal transition in glioblastoma, suggesting NPY downregulation may be a marker of this aggressive shift. The finding integrates bulk RNA-seq data from TCGA, GTEx, and validation cohorts to characterize NPY's role in GBM progression.

papers.title: PLNFGL: Joint Estimation of Multi-Condition Gene Networks from Single-cell RNA-seq Data.
papers.abstract: Graphical models have been widely used in bioinformatics to infer the conditional dependence structure among random variables, but traditional Gaussian graphical models (GGMs) are suboptimal for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) due to dropout events and distributional mismatch. Moreover, most existing methods estimate networks under a single condition, limiting their utility in multi-condition studies.
papers.summary: This paper introduces PLNFGL, a method that jointly estimates gene regulatory networks across multiple conditions from single-cell RNA-seq data, addressing challenges like dropout events and distributional mismatch that make traditional Gaussian graphical models unsuitable. By modeling multiple conditions simultaneously, it improves network inference and enables comparative analysis of gene networks across different biological states.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomics in Breast Cancer: Advances and Applications.
papers.abstract: While traditional transcriptomics and single-cell RNA sequencing can reveal differences in cell type and gene expression, they cannot provide spatial information within tissues. Spatial transcriptomics (ST), as an emerging technology in recent years, has achieved significant progress in resolving gene expression along the spatial dimension. This technology quantifies gene expression at defined spatial coordinates and describes the spatial distribution of transcripts and the co-localization patterns between cells within intact tissue, allowing for an integrated analysis of molecular and spatial
papers.summary: This review discusses how spatial transcriptomics (ST) overcomes limitations of traditional and single-cell RNA sequencing by preserving spatial context within tissues. It highlights ST's ability to map gene expression to specific coordinates, revealing cellular co-localization patterns and enabling integrated molecular-spatial analysis in breast cancer research.

papers.title: Identifying condition-related cell-cell communication events using supervised tensor analysis.
papers.abstract: Many tools have been developed to infer active cell-cell communication (CCC) events, which are essential for understanding biological processes and diseases. However, existing methods for assessing the relationships between CCC events and biological conditions have at least one practical limitation: a lack of clear interpretation, an inability to adjust for confounders, or an inability to model inherent dependencies among CCC events. To comprehensively address these limitations, we introduce STACCato, a supervised tensor analysis tool for identifying condition-related CCC events. STACCato empl
papers.summary: This paper introduces STACCato, a new tool that uses supervised tensor analysis to identify cell-cell communication events related to specific biological conditions. Unlike existing methods, STACCato addresses key limitations by providing clear interpretation, adjusting for confounders, and modeling dependencies among communication events.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomics of Immune Cell Distribution in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Identifies Tertiary Lymphoid Structures and Its Density and Area Fraction Were Associated with Neoadjuvant Therapy Response.
papers.abstract: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) remains one of the leading causes of cancer-related mortality worldwide over the past decade. Single-cell sequencing loses spatial location information and potential cell-cell interactions, making it difficult to interpret molecular features or biological phenomena. Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) inherently require such spatial immune cell distribution information. Although associations between TLS and response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) or chemotherapy have been reported, the relationship between TLS and neoadjuvant therapy (ICI combined wit
papers.summary: This study uses spatial transcriptomics to map immune cell distribution in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), focusing on tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs). The authors find that TLS density and area fraction are associated with response to neoadjuvant therapy, highlighting the importance of spatial organization in treatment outcomes.

papers.title: A pan-cancer single-cell atlas uncovers the role of sex hormones and chromosomes in sex-divergent reprogramming of the tumor microenvironment.
papers.abstract: Sex bias is pervasive in tumors; however, how sex chromosomes and hormone-responsive signaling shape the tumor microenvironment (TME) remains insufficiently characterized. Considering the critical impact of the TME on tumor progression and response to immunotherapy, a pan-cancer investigation of sex-specific and cancer-context-dependent TME features is warranted.
papers.summary: This study creates a pan-cancer single-cell atlas to investigate how sex hormones and chromosomes contribute to differences in the tumor microenvironment between males and females. The findings reveal sex-divergent reprogramming of the TME, which may influence tumor progression and immunotherapy responses.

papers.title: PRECISE: A Prognostic Thyrocyte-Derived Gene Signature for Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) exhibits heterogeneous behavior, underscoring the need for effective biomarkers and improved risk stratification methods. We developed a thyrocyte-derived gene signature, Prognostic RNA Expression Cell-specific Integrated Signature (PRECISE), using single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) and evaluated its prognostic utility across cohorts.
papers.journal: Clinical cancer research : an official journal of the American Association for Cancer Research
papers.summary: Researchers developed a new gene signature called PRECISE from single-cell RNA sequencing data to better predict outcomes in papillary thyroid carcinoma. This signature, derived from thyrocytes, improved risk stratification across multiple patient cohorts. The work addresses the need for better biomarkers in this heterogeneous cancer.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomic Atlas Reveals That Forkhead Box O3-Mediated Mitochondrial Dynamics Imbalance Drives Premature Ovarian Insufficiency in Mice.
papers.abstract: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is a major driver of female reproductive aging, but its mechanisms and the spatial and structural patterns of reproductive aging remain poorly understood. This study, therefore, constructed a spatial transcriptomic atlas of POI mouse models to define the spatial and molecular features of granulosa senescence during disease progression. Spatial analysis revealed disrupted follicular structure and distinct granulosa subpopulations exhibiting blocked differentiation and senescence-associated gene signatures. Integrating multiple gene sets identified structura
papers.summary: This study created a spatial transcriptomic atlas of premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) in mice, revealing that disrupted follicular structure and distinct granulosa cell subpopulations with blocked differentiation and senescence signatures drive the condition. The findings implicate Forkhead Box O3-mediated mitochondrial dynamics imbalance as a key mechanism underlying reproductive aging.

papers.title: GAHIB: graph attention VAE with a hyperbolic information bottleneck for biologically structured single-cell representations.
papers.abstract: Current single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) variational autoencoders (VAEs) usually emphasise local cell graph structure, hyperbolic latent geometry, or bottleneck compression separately, yet these biases are rarely evaluated together in one evidence-gated representation model. We present GAHIB (graph attention VAE with a hyperbolic information bottleneck), which combines a graph-attention encoder, a 2D information bottleneck, and a Lorentz-hyperbolic geometry loss. Because the main clustering benchmark uses Leiden-derived proxy labels rather than definitive biological ground truth, we eval
papers.summary: GAHIB is a new variational autoencoder that simultaneously integrates graph attention, a hyperbolic latent space, and an information bottleneck to better represent single-cell RNA-seq data. The model aims to capture both local cell-cell relationships and global hierarchical structure, though its evaluation relies on computational clustering rather than true biological labels.

papers.title: Discovery of hub genes linking oxidative stress to type 2 diabetic sarcopenia using single-cell sequencing and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) and sarcopenia demonstrate a significant comorbidity, particularly in the elderly, yet the molecular mechanisms linking them, especially through oxidative stress, remain incompletely understood. This study aimed to identify oxidative stress-related hub genes involved in T2DM-associated sarcopenia (T2DS) by integrating single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and bulk RNA-seq data with machine learning. We analyzed scRNA-seq datasets (GSE244515, GSE268953) to characterize cellular heterogeneity and bulk RNA-seq datasets (GSE202295, GSE226151) for differential expre
papers.summary: This study identifies key genes linking oxidative stress to sarcopenia in type 2 diabetes by combining single-cell and bulk RNA sequencing with machine learning. The findings reveal potential molecular targets for understanding and treating muscle loss in diabetic patients.

papers.title: Single-Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics Identify the Mono-S100A4 Subset Associated with Sepsis-Induced Lung Injury via the MIF-(CD74+CD44) Axis.
papers.abstract: In sepsis-induced immune dysregulation, monocytes contribute critically through their dual pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive functions. Leading to multi-organ injury, the lungs are the most vulnerable initial target, dictating clinical outcomes. Thus, studying monocyte dynamics is essential to understand sepsis-induced acute lung injury (ALI) pathophysiology.
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to identify a specific monocyte subset (Mono-S100A4) that is associated with sepsis-induced lung injury. The subset appears to contribute to lung damage through the MIF-(CD74+CD44) signaling axis, providing a potential target for therapeutic intervention.

papers.title: GxP-Ready Single-Cell RNA-seq and Spatial Transcriptomics End-to-End Pipeline for Clinical Research.
papers.abstract: Single-cell/nucleus RNA-sequencing and Spatial Transcriptomics are powerful tools for investigating cellular heterogeneity and tissue architecture that have deepened our disease understanding. Their broader adoption in clinical and regulated settings, however, is hindered by regulatory requirements related to data integrity, regulatory compliance, reproducibility, and scalability. To address this gap, we developed NNclinSSOAP (Novo Nordisk Clinical Single-cell Spatial Omics Analytical Pipeline)-a modular, GxP-ready end-to-end computational pipeline that combines established single-cell workflo
papers.summary: This paper presents NNclinSSOAP, a modular computational pipeline designed to make single-cell RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics analyses compliant with GxP regulatory standards for clinical research. The pipeline integrates established workflows to address challenges in data integrity, reproducibility, and scalability, aiming to facilitate the adoption of these technologies in regulated settings.

papers.title: Multi-Omics Analysis Reveals Nono-Kcnq2 Regulation of Neuronal Excitability in Chronic Constriction Injury-Induced Neuropathic Pain.
papers.abstract: Neuropathic pain, resulting from somatosensory nervous system damage or disease, is a debilitating condition marked by spontaneous pain, hypersensitivity, and sensory abnormalities. Neuropathic pain involves spinal neuronal hyperexcitability, yet its molecular basis remains poorly defined. We utilized a comprehensive multi-omics approach, incorporating proteomics, phosphoproteomics, concatenated tandem array of consensus transcription response elements, and both single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, to chart time-resolved adaptations in the L4 to L6 spinal cord of a chronic constriction inj
papers.summary: This study uses a multi-omics approach (proteomics, phosphoproteomics, and transcriptomics) to map molecular changes in the spinal cord during neuropathic pain, identifying the Nono-Kcnq2 pathway as a key regulator of neuronal excitability. The findings provide new insights into the molecular basis of spinal neuron hyperexcitability in chronic pain.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics analysis implicates the RNF40-LIMA1 axis in hepatocellular carcinoma progression and immune microenvironment remodeling.
papers.abstract: Ring Finger Protein 40 (RNF40), an E3 ubiquitin ligase, plays context-dependent roles in various cancers, serving as either a tumor suppressor or an oncogene. Critically, RNF40 targets LIM domain and actin-binding protein 1 (LIMA1) for degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. LIMA1, which is involved in actin cytoskeleton organization, exerts opposing effects in different cancer types. However, the precise functional relationship between RNF40 and LIMA1 in Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) remains poorly understood. Despite the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors, intrinsic resistan
papers.summary: This study identifies the RNF40-LIMA1 axis as a key regulator in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) progression and immune microenvironment remodeling through integrative multi-omics analysis. RNF40 promotes HCC by degrading LIMA1 via ubiquitination, and this axis correlates with immune cell infiltration and response to checkpoint inhibitors. The findings suggest RNF40 and LIMA1 as potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers for HCC immunotherapy.

papers.title: Spatial RNA velocity reveals cellular state transitions and prognostic markers in the melanoma microenvironment.
papers.abstract: The inability to infer transcriptional dynamics from high-resolution spatial transcriptomics represents a major computational challenge, as these datasets lack the spliced/unspliced mRNA counts required for conventional RNA velocity. To address this, we developed a novel computational framework that repurposes subcellular transcript localization-using nuclear and cytoplasmic RNAs as proxies for unspliced and spliced mRNA, respectively-for spatial RNA velocity analysis. By integrating this approach with scVelo's dynamical model, we inferred directional state transitions directly from melanoma s
papers.summary: This paper introduces a computational framework that uses subcellular RNA localization (nuclear vs. cytoplasmic) as a proxy for unspliced/spliced mRNA to infer RNA velocity from spatial transcriptomics data. Applied to melanoma, the method successfully predicts cellular state transitions and identifies prognostic markers, addressing a key limitation of spatial transcriptomics.

papers.title: SPP1+ Macrophages Promote Immunosuppression in Post-TACE Hepatocellular Carcinoma Involving Lactate-Induced Lactylation and Potential SPP1-CD44 Signaling.
papers.abstract: Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) treats inoperable liver cancer by inducing tumor ischemia, but subsequent hypoxia can promote an immunosuppressive microenvironment. This study explores how TACE-related hypoxia influences immune cells through lactate metabolism and lactylation.
papers.summary: This study reveals that after TACE treatment for liver cancer, hypoxia leads to increased lactate production, which promotes immunosuppressive SPP1+ macrophages via lactylation. These macrophages then suppress anti-tumor immunity through SPP1-CD44 signaling, suggesting a mechanism for TACE resistance.
